hook_patterns: Promise of 'crazy and bizarre facts' + subject name in first 3 words
Exclusivity claim: 'you won't learn anywhere else'
Lead with most shocking/taboo element first (mommy's boy, brainwashing)
Immediate humanizing flaw of legendary figure (undermines mythology)
structural_beats: BEAT 1: Psychological/personal flaw that contradicts public image (mommy's boy vs conqueror)
BEAT 2: Famous challenge/test story with unexpected solution (Gordian knot → brute force hack)
BEAT 3: Death/finale twist that reframes everything (buried alive reveal)
Pattern: Chronological through life BUT selecting only counterintuitive moments
Each beat is self-contained mini-story (3-4 sentences max)
Escalating stakes: personal flaw → legendary moment → ultimate fate
rhetorical_devices: Conversational interjections: 'I guess he really bought that'
Problem-solution framing with casual dismissal: 'problem solved'
Contrast pairs: 'proving he was a God' vs 'he wasn't dead to begin with'
Authority layering: 'Greek Scholars wrote' → 'modern day scientists believe' (ancient vs contemporary)
Dramatic irony: 'History's Greatest conqueror got buried alive' (ultimate reversal)
Present tense for immediacy: 'he then drew his sword out'
Cliffhanger phrasing: 'in reality that was because...' (creates micro-suspense)
vocabulary_motifs: Definitive historical labels: 'History's Greatest conqueror', 'most famous case'
Mystical/elevated language for setup: 'shrouded in prophecy', 'warrior king'
Clinical/scientific language for debunking: 'neurological disorder', 'Physicians declared'
Colloquial deflation: 'Mommy's boy', 'he really bought that', 'problem solved'
pacing_notes: Rapid-fire delivery with NO filler. Sentences range 8-20 words. Three distinct story blocks with hard cuts between them (no transitions). Info density is extreme: every sentence contains a fact + interpretation. Speeds up toward end (final sentence is longest but delivers maximum shock). No breathing room—assumes viewer will rewatch. Comma splices used intentionally for breathless momentum.
payoff_patterns: Final line delivers darkest irony/reversal of entire narrative
Callback to opening label ('History's Greatest conqueror') but subverted
Ends on visceral image ('buried alive') not explanation
No moral or lesson—just leaves viewer with disturbing reframe
Payoff is recontextualization: everything you thought you knew is wrong
topics: Alexander the Great's relationship with his mother Olympias
Zeus paternity myth and divine right ideology
Gordian Knot legend and Alexander's pragmatic solution
Modern medical theories: neurological disorder causing paralysis misdiagnosed as death
do_not_do: Never use academic hedging ('perhaps', 'it's possible', 'some historians think')
Never explain context or background (assume viewer knows who Alexander is)
Never use transition phrases ('moving on', 'next', 'another interesting fact')
Never end with summary or call-to-action
Never present facts neutrally—always frame with emotional angle (shocking/ironic/dark)
Never slow down to let information sink in
Never cite specific sources (use vague authority: 'Greek Scholars', 'modern scientists')
